title: What is an AI Agent?
summary: Traces the history of AI agents from early academic definitions to the 2023 ReAct breakthrough that changed everything. Establishes what "agentic AI" actually means versus older AI concepts.
teaching_points: AI agents are autonomous systems that perceive their environments, pursue goals over time, and adapt to change — a definition from Russell & Norvig (1995) that still holds.
The field divides into Pre-ReAct and ReAct eras. The 2023 ReAct paper (Shunyu Yao, Princeton/Google) unified reasoning and acting in a single loop, making practical agents buildable.
ReAct architecture requires LLMs to output tool messages in a specific format — Thought → Action → Observation — enabling structured agent behavior.
Scale matters: Craig Reynolds' 1986 'boids' showed that simple agent rules produce emergent behavior greater than the sum of their parts. Multi-agent systems follow the same principle.
After 2023, a wave of platforms (LangChain, LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen) emerged all building on ReAct fundamentals — so understanding ReAct means understanding all of them.
common_confusion: ReAct is not a framework — it's an architecture pattern. LangChain and LangGraph implement it. Learning ReAct means you can reason about any agent platform.

title: LLM Foundations for Agent Builders
summary: What you need to know about how LLMs work before building agents — training stages, reasoning vs acting, and why token prediction gives rise to apparently intelligent behavior.
teaching_points: LLMs go through pre-training (learning language patterns from massive text corpora) and post-training (fine-tuning via RLHF/DPO to be helpful and aligned). You interact with post-trained models.
Reasoning in an LLM emerges from training — it is not explicit logic. When an LLM 'reasons', it is generating likely next tokens based on patterns, not running a theorem prover.
Acting (the 'Act' in ReAct) means outputting structured tool calls. The LLM knows when to call a tool because its output format is constrained: Thought → Action → Observation → repeat.
The ReAct loop: the LLM produces a Thought (reasoning step), then an Action (tool call), receives an Observation (tool result), and repeats until it produces a final Answer.
Knowing this helps you debug agents: if an agent loops infinitely, the LLM's reasoning chain is not converging; if it fails to call tools, the output format is wrong.
common_confusion: Post-training alignment doesn't mean the model 'understands' — it means its outputs are shaped to be useful and safe. The underlying mechanism is still next-token prediction.

title: Building with LangChain
summary: Practical introduction to LangChain's ecosystem: how Python packages, virtual environments, API keys, and the LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) fit together to build agent pipelines.
teaching_points: LangChain is a Python package that wraps LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) with a unified interface for building chains, tools, and agents. Install via pip in a virtual environment.
API keys are credentials passed as environment variables (never hardcoded). LangChain reads them via os.getenv() or a .env file. Always rotate exposed keys.
LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) uses the pipe operator | to chain components: prompt | model | output_parser. This is the core composition pattern.
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is the most common LangChain pattern: load documents → chunk → embed → store in vector DB → retrieve on query → pass to LLM as context.
The three main components to know: Prompts (templates), Models (LLM wrappers), and Tools (functions the agent can call). Everything else builds on these.
common_confusion: LangChain's RAG implementations all follow the same core pattern: retrieve then augment. The various 'RAG variants' (HyDE, Hybrid RAG, etc.) are optimizations of retrieval quality, not different architectures.

title: Middleware & Tool Integration
summary: Middleware is a LangChain-specific mechanism (not part of ReAct) that lets you intercept and modify agent behavior at each step. Key for control, logging, and safety guardrails.
teaching_points: Middleware is a LangChain abstraction (not in the original ReAct paper) that sits between steps in an agent's execution, allowing interception and modification of inputs/outputs.
Built-in middleware handles common cases (rate limiting, retry logic, tracing). Custom middleware lets you implement any control logic: content filtering, logging, human approval gates.
Execution order matters: middleware is applied in the order it is passed. This resembles middleware stacks in web frameworks (Express, Django).
Middleware is only available in LangChain and Microsoft's agent framework — not CrewAI or AutoGen. It's a LangChain-specific design pattern.
Use middleware for observability: wrapping agent calls with logging middleware is how you build a trace of every step for debugging or governance.
common_confusion: Middleware is not the same as tools. Tools are what the agent calls to act on the world. Middleware wraps the agent's execution pipeline itself.

title: Advanced LangChain: RAG & Memory
summary: Deep dive into RAG patterns, short-term and long-term memory, and HITL control in single-agent systems. Also covers structured output schemas for tools.
teaching_points: Advanced RAG: the core pattern (retrieve → augment → generate) stays constant across all variants. Hybrid RAG combines vector search with keyword search (BM25) for better recall.
Short-term memory = the conversation window the LLM can see. Long-term memory = persisted storage retrieved by similarity search. In 2026, long-term memory techniques are still maturing.
HITL (Human-in-the-Loop) in LangChain: interrupt the agent chain at a checkpointed step, surface the agent's proposed action to a human, and resume only after approval.
Structured output schemas force the LLM to return JSON matching a Pydantic model. This is how you build reliable tool integrations — instead of parsing free text, you validate structured data.
Multimodality: passing images, audio, or documents to the LLM as context alongside text. The agent can then reason about non-text inputs. Supported in GPT-4o and Claude.
common_confusion: RAG is often described as 'dead' in favor of long-context models. But long-context models still benefit from RAG for cost efficiency and retrieval precision — the two approaches complement each other.

title: Intro to LangGraph
summary: LangGraph extends LangChain with graph-based orchestration: nodes (agent steps), edges (transitions), and stateful state objects that persist across the entire workflow.
teaching_points: LangGraph models agent workflows as a directed graph: nodes are processing steps (LLM calls, tool calls, conditions), edges define transitions between nodes.
The key insight: graphs let you represent branching, looping, and parallel execution explicitly — things that are awkward to express as a linear chain.
Workflow → Agentic Workflow → Agent: a Workflow is a fixed sequence; an Agentic Workflow uses an LLM to decide transitions; an Agent fully controls its own execution path.
SubGraphs as Nodes: you can nest a compiled graph inside a node of a parent graph. This is how you build modular multi-agent systems — each agent is a subgraph.
The Graph API has three parts: Nodes (define), Edges (connect), Compilation (build). Compile the graph once, then invoke it with a state object.
common_confusion: LangGraph is not a replacement for LangChain — it extends it. LangGraph handles the orchestration layer (when to call what); LangChain handles the component layer (how to call it).

title: Advanced LangGraph
summary: Production-grade LangGraph: app structure for local deployment, interrupt functions for fine-grained human control, and integrating LangGraph with a chat UI.
teaching_points: LangGraph app structure for local deployment: a compiled graph + a FastAPI server + a LangGraph Studio UI. The studio lets you visualize graph execution in real time.
Interrupts are more flexible than HITL: instead of a binary pause/resume, interrupts let you inject values, modify state, or choose a different execution path at any node.
Super-steps: in LangGraph, nodes in the same super-step execute in parallel. This is how you build fan-out/fan-in patterns for concurrent agent execution.
Persistence: LangGraph checkpointers save state between steps. This enables long-running agents that can be paused and resumed, even across process restarts.
Agent Chat UI: LangGraph integrates with streaming front-ends. The agent streams tokens as it generates, giving the user real-time feedback rather than waiting for the full response.
common_confusion: Interrupts and HITL are related but different: HITL is a design pattern (human reviews agent output); interrupts are the LangGraph mechanism that implements it — and more.

title: Deep Agents & Frontier Topics
summary: Frontier architectures: Deep Agent systems with subagents, agent skills, multi-modal agents, and the open research problems in long-term memory and agent reliability.
teaching_points: Deep Agent architecture: a main agent delegates to specialized subagents. The main agent handles high-level planning and routing; subagents handle domain-specific tasks. This is how production systems scale.
Agent Skills (Anthropic's term): the agent calls a tool to load a 'skill' — a bundle of instructions and capabilities — dynamically. Enables agents to acquire new capabilities at runtime.
Multi-modal agents: agents that can receive images, audio, and documents, not just text. The agent reasons across modalities to plan and act — e.g., screenshot → identify UI element → click.
Long-term memory research frontier: current approaches use vector DBs for semantic retrieval, but temporal ordering, memory consolidation, and forgetting are still unsolved.
The hardest open problems: agents that reliably know when to stop, agents that catch their own errors, and agents that can be trusted with high-stakes decisions without supervision.
common_confusion: Multi-agent ≠ better. Adding more agents increases complexity and failure modes. Use the minimal agent design that solves the problem reliably.

title: Data Understanding & Storytelling
summary: Why good analytics starts with good data visualization. Covers EDA, choosing the right chart, and how data presentation choices affect the conclusions your audience draws.
teaching_points: Good data analytics is good storytelling. Before running any model, ask: what question am I answering, and for whom?
A simple picture can tell a powerful story. Complexity in a chart is often a sign of unclear thinking, not rigorous analysis.
With smaller populations, rates are more volatile — don't confuse variance with signal. Always contextualize metrics by population size.
Pre-attentive attributes (color, size, position) guide the reader's eye. Use them deliberately to highlight what matters, not to decorate.
EDA is not optional. Models built without exploratory analysis routinely fail in predictable ways — outliers, data entry errors, and distribution skew that should have been caught first.
common_confusion: EDA ≠ descriptive statistics. Summary stats (mean, std) collapse distribution shape. Always plot your data before summarizing it numerically.

title: Regression Analysis in Practice
summary: Hands-on regression: building models in R, interpreting coefficients, testing significance, evaluating fit, and understanding when regression predictions are reliable.
teaching_points: Multiple linear regression: y = β₀ + β₁x₁ + ... + βₙxₙ + ε. Each βᵢ is the expected change in y for a one-unit increase in xᵢ, holding other variables constant.
Significance testing via t-tests and F-test: the t-test evaluates each coefficient; the F-test evaluates the model as a whole. Both are needed for a complete picture.
Standardized coefficients (beta weights) let you compare the relative importance of predictors measured on different scales.
Prediction intervals vs confidence intervals: CI estimates where the mean response falls; PI estimates where a single new observation falls. PIs are always wider.
Train-test split: always evaluate prediction performance on held-out data you did not use for fitting. In-sample R² is flattering; out-of-sample RMSE is honest.
common_confusion: R² measures how well the model fits training data, not how well it predicts. A high R² with poor out-of-sample predictions means you overfit.

title: Supervised Learning Foundations
summary: The conceptual backbone of MBAN 5560: bias-variance tradeoff, parametric vs nonparametric models, and systematic model selection via grid search and cross-validation.
teaching_points: Bias-variance tradeoff: simple models underfit (high bias), complex models overfit (high variance). The goal is finding the sweet spot on the complexity axis for your data.

Parametric models (linear regression, logistic regression) assume a functional form; nonparametric models (kNN, trees) let the data define the shape. Parametric models are more interpretable but less flexible.
Grid search: systematically evaluate all combinations of hyperparameter values on a validation set. Computationally expensive but exhaustive.
Cross-validation: split data into k folds, train on k-1 folds, validate on the held-out fold, rotate k times. Averages performance across folds for a more robust estimate than a single split.
The overfitting diagnostic: training error keeps dropping as complexity increases; validation error eventually rises. The minimum of the validation curve is your target complexity.
common_confusion: Cross-validation selects hyperparameters (model selection); the test set evaluates the final model (model assessment). Never use the test set for selection — that is data leakage.

title: kNN, Clustering & Resampling
summary: k-Nearest Neighbors for classification and regression, K-means and DBSCAN for clustering, and bootstrap resampling for uncertainty quantification.
teaching_points: kNN classifies a new point by finding the k nearest training points (by distance) and taking a majority vote. For regression, it averages their values.
Choosing k: small k = low bias, high variance (memorizes noise); large k = high bias, low variance (over-smooths). Use cross-validation to select k.
K-means clustering: assign points to the nearest centroid, recompute centroids, repeat until convergence. Results depend on initialization — run multiple times with different seeds.
Bootstrap: resample with replacement from your training data B times, fit a model each time, aggregate results. Gives you an estimate of model variance without needing more data.
DBSCAN identifies clusters as dense regions, automatically detects outliers as noise points, and doesn't require you to pre-specify the number of clusters.
common_confusion: kNN's weakness is scale sensitivity — features on larger scales dominate the distance calculation. Always normalize features before running kNN.

title: Decision Trees & Ensemble Methods
summary: CART trees, the Gini impurity criterion, and how bagging and boosting turn weak trees into powerful ensemble models (Random Forest, GBM, XGBoost).
teaching_points: CART splits each node by finding the variable and threshold that minimizes Gini impurity (classification) or MSE (regression) in the resulting child nodes.
Gini impurity G ≈ 0 means a node is pure (all one class); G = 0.5 means perfectly mixed. At each split, CART finds the split that minimizes the weighted average Gini of children.
Single trees overfit badly. The fix: ensembles. Bagging (Random Forest) trains many trees on bootstrap samples and averages. Boosting (GBM, XGBoost) trains trees sequentially to correct prior errors.
Random Forest adds feature randomness: each tree only sees a random subset of features at each split. This de-correlates the trees, reducing ensemble variance.
XGBoost is gradient boosting with regularization, parallelization, and missing-value handling built in. It dominates tabular data competitions for a reason.
common_confusion: Random Forest and Gradient Boosting both use trees, but differently: RF trains trees independently and averages; GBM trains trees sequentially where each corrects the last.

title: Neural Networks & Deep Learning
summary: From single-layer perceptrons to deep networks: how ANNs approximate arbitrary functions, the role of activation functions, and backpropagation as the learning algorithm.
teaching_points: An ANN is a layered function: input layer → hidden layers → output layer. Each neuron applies a weighted sum of its inputs followed by a nonlinear activation function.
Activation functions introduce nonlinearity that lets the network approximate complex functions. ReLU (max(0,x)) is the standard choice for hidden layers; softmax for multi-class output.
Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight by applying the chain rule backward through the network. Gradient descent then updates weights.
Architecture choices drive performance: depth (more layers = more abstract features), width (more neurons = more capacity), and regularization (dropout, L2) control overfitting.
Deep learning (CNNs for images, RNNs/LSTMs for sequences, Transformers for text) applies the same ANN principle with architectures matched to the data's structure.
common_confusion: More layers ≠ better. Very deep networks suffer from vanishing gradients (signals shrink to zero during backprop). Residual connections (ResNets) solve this.

title: Bias, Reasoning & Motivated Cognition
summary: Why humans make biased decisions — and why AI inherits and amplifies those biases. Covers Kahneman's System 1/2 thinking, motivated reasoning, and cognitive bias taxonomy.
teaching_points: Kahneman's System 1/2: System 1 is fast, automatic, associative — handles routine decisions. System 2 is slow, deliberate, logical — handles novel problems. Most of the time, System 1 runs.
Motivated reasoning: we construct justifications for conclusions we already want to reach. This is particularly dangerous when evaluating AI outputs that confirm our priors.
AI systems encode the biases of their training data. Data bias, algorithmic bias, and deployment bias are distinct failure modes that require different interventions.
Anchoring bias: the first piece of information encountered disproportionately influences all subsequent judgments. AI recommendations become anchors that human reviewers may not sufficiently adjust.
The defend-then-critique framework: force yourself to defend a position you disagree with before critiquing it. This is the course's primary methodology for building intellectual flexibility.
common_confusion: Bias in AI is not just a 'fairness' problem — it is a reliability problem. A biased model is an inaccurate model for the populations that are underrepresented in training data.

title: Ethics Frameworks & Governance
summary: Classical ethics frameworks applied to AI governance: Aristotelian virtue ethics, Kantian deontology, and social contract theory, plus professional codes and regulatory frameworks.
teaching_points: Virtue ethics asks: what would a person of good character do? Applied to AI: what values should be embedded in system design, not just compliance rules?
Kantian ethics (deontology): act only according to rules you could will to be universal laws. Applied to AI: never treat people merely as means to an end, even for greater aggregate benefit.
Social contract theory: legitimate authority derives from agreements among those it governs. AI governance requires consent and accountability to those affected by AI decisions.
Professional codes (like Sarbanes-Oxley, EU CSR directives) translate ethical principles into legal obligations. Knowing the law matters but does not substitute for ethical judgment.

The governance overlay in this toolkit: 8 risk dimensions (interpretability, validation, fairness, privacy, traceability, HITL need, deployment risk, misuse risk) operationalize ethics into checkable criteria.
common_confusion: Compliance ≠ ethics. An action can be legal but unethical, and organizations that conflate them create governance gaps that eventually produce failures.

title: Python Programming for Analytics
summary: Building Python proficiency from the ground up: scripting, OOP, pandas data manipulation, and applying Python to run and replicate operations management experiments.
teaching_points: Python scripting basics: variables, loops, functions, file I/O. Jupyter Notebooks are the primary medium for analytical Python work — mix code, output, and explanation in one document.

Object-oriented programming (OOP): classes bundle data (attributes) and behavior (methods). In analytics, you typically consume OOP designs (pandas DataFrames, sklearn models) more than build your own.
Pandas is the core tool for data manipulation: loading CSVs, filtering rows, grouping, aggregating, merging. The DataFrame is the central data structure.
Linear regression in Python via scikit-learn or statsmodels. The course used Python to replicate operations management experiments from academic papers.
Experimental replication methodology: reproduce a published result using the same data and method, then verify your code produces the same output. Builds rigor and catches implementation errors.
common_confusion: No lecture slides extracted — content derived from project artifacts and assignment briefs.

title: Linear Programming & Optimization
summary: Prescriptive analytics: formulating LP problems, solving with Excel Solver and Gurobi, interpreting shadow prices, and understanding duality theory.
teaching_points: LP formulation process: (1) define decision variables, (2) write the objective function, (3) write constraints, (4) add non-negativity. This sequence is the same for every problem.
Excel Solver and Gurobi both solve LPs, but Gurobi (Python) scales to large problems and supports integer programming out of the box. Excel Solver is for learning and small-scale use.
Shadow price (dual variable): the change in objective value per unit relaxation of a constraint. If the shadow price on a labor constraint is $5, adding one hour of labor increases profit by $5.
Sensitivity analysis: how much can a coefficient change before the optimal solution changes? The allowable range gives you the stability window for your recommendation.
Duality: every LP has a 'mirror' problem (the dual). Primal solution and dual solution give you the same optimal value. The dual variables are the shadow prices.
common_confusion: No lecture slides extracted — content derived from exam prep materials and assignment briefs (provisional reconstruction).

title: Integer Programming & Decision Analysis
summary: When LP is not enough: binary and mixed-integer programming for yes/no decisions, plus decision analysis under uncertainty using expected monetary value and EVPI.
teaching_points: Integer programming adds integrality constraints: decision variables must be integers (or binary). This makes problems NP-hard but models real binary decisions (build/don't build, hire/don't hire).
Binary IP applications: the knapsack problem (what to pack given weight limit), assignment problem (who does which task), transportation problem (how to route shipments).
Decision analysis under uncertainty: use a decision tree. Expected Monetary Value (EMV) = Σ(probability × payoff). Choose the branch with the highest EMV.
EVPI (Expected Value of Perfect Information) = EMV with perfect info − EMV without. It is the maximum you should pay for a forecast or study.

Multi-objective optimization: when you have two objectives (cost and quality), trace the Pareto frontier — no solution is strictly better on both dimensions. Decision-makers choose a point on the frontier.
common_confusion: EMV maximization assumes risk neutrality. If the decision-maker is risk-averse, use utility theory — the utility of a payoff is not proportional to its dollar value.

title: Relational Database Design
summary: Building relational databases from scratch: ER modelling, normalization, schema design, and writing SQL queries to extract business intelligence from operational data.
teaching_points: ER diagrams model business entities (Customer, Reservation, Ship) and the relationships between them (Customer MAKES Reservation) before any SQL is written.
Normalization removes redundancy: 1NF (no repeating groups), 2NF (no partial dependencies), 3NF (no transitive dependencies). Normalized schemas reduce update anomalies.
SQL query writing: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, JOIN, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY. Joins connect tables via foreign keys — understanding join types (INNER, LEFT, OUTER) is essential.
KPI computation via SQL: aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX) with GROUP BY. The Cruise Line project required computing occupancy rates, revenue per booking, and fleet utilization.

Schema design decisions have long-term consequences: a poorly designed schema makes queries harder, slower, and more error-prone. Getting the ER diagram right before building saves significant rework.
common_confusion: No lecture slides extracted — content derived from the Cruise Line Database Project and SQL assignment artifacts.

title: Financial Risk & Monte Carlo Simulation
summary: Quantifying uncertainty in financial decisions using @RISK Monte Carlo simulation, scenario analysis, and pro forma forecasting under uncertain assumptions.
teaching_points: @RISK Monte Carlo: replace point estimates in Excel models with probability distributions. Run thousands of simulations to get a distribution of outcomes, not a single number.
Pro forma financial statements: income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement projected forward using assumptions. The 3-statement dynamic model links all three.
NPV under uncertainty: run Monte Carlo on the discount rate and cash flow assumptions to get a distribution of NPVs. The probability that NPV > 0 is your go/no-go confidence.
Decision trees (Precision Tree): model sequential decisions under uncertainty. At each node, choose the branch that maximizes expected utility — same logic as EMV in MBAN 5540.
Risk communication: a distribution of outcomes is harder to communicate than a single number. Translate to managerial language: 'there is a 20% chance of losing more than $1M'.
common_confusion: No lecture slides extracted — content derived from course outline and assignment briefs.

title: Equity Research & AI-Assisted Analysis
summary: Equity research methodology: fundamental analysis, AI-assisted financial analysis, and the critical evaluation framework for knowing when to trust (and when to override) AI outputs.
teaching_points: Equity research process: company overview → industry analysis → financial statement analysis → valuation → investment recommendation. Each step informs the next.
Valuation models: DCF (discounted cash flow), comparable company analysis (comps), and precedent transaction analysis. DCF requires forecasting free cash flows and a discount rate.
AI-assisted analysis: use LLMs to accelerate literature review, earnings call summarization, and sentiment analysis. But AI pattern recognition can miss structural breaks and economic reasoning.

The AI evaluation framework (from course): (1) verify AI-generated claims against source data, (2) stress-test AI recommendations with opposing scenarios, (3) retain human judgment for non-routine decisions.
Event study methodology: measure the abnormal return around a corporate event (earnings announcement, M&A). Separates event impact from market-wide noise using the CAPM residual.
common_confusion: AI-assisted analysis does not replace judgment — it accelerates research on routine tasks. The value-add is in the critical evaluation layer on top, not the AI output itself.
